source.notes: Optimus _ BM2 (trial review)  - 2026_03_30 13_59 EDT - Notes by Gemini.docx

call_summary: Trial-review call between Optimus (Ed Stockman, Max Riemer) and freight brokerage BM2 (Talmadge Studer and 'Jay'/Jess). Trial feedback was strongly positive — reps found the transportation data far more valuable than minor contact-data gaps versus ZoomInfo — but the deal is blocked by BM2's existing annual ZoomInfo contract (~$20–25k, Feb-to-Feb) that would force double-paying. Ed floated a reduced fee (~$250–500/mo) plus a gain-share model tied to a TMS integration, which surfaced data-sharing concerns he offered to solve with contract language; the agreed path is for Talmadge to pursue an early ZoomInfo exit while Optimus emails pricing options.

delivery_engagement.explanation: Ed was clearly attentive and built directly on what the buyer said rather than reciting a script, mirroring Talmadge's 'data outweighs contacts' logic and immediately asking for specifics to act on; acoustic arousal was steady-to-rising across thirds [0.488, 0.51, 0.518] with positive valence.

evidence.quote: I'm so curious on some of those names and contacts. Um, if you can send us over any examples, I I will dive into those in a heartbeat.
Like, that stuff keeps me up at night. Like, I obsess over that stuff.
And that's that for us, like we've weeded out a lot of like bad partners because of that.

value_prop_clarity.explanation: Ed articulated a concrete, quantified proposition: a low fixed fee plus gain-share, with the platform proactively surfacing warm leads matched to BM2's own network and auto-writing value-prop emails to help reps close more deals.

evidence.quote: What if we did um what if we proposed something very minimal like 250 or 500 bucks a month and gain share.
we'll start proactively pushing them alerts. The shipper matches your customer. This is a good lead.
It's going to look at the shippers network, figure out what that right value proposition is, and write that email for that sales rep.

relevance.explanation: The pitch was tailored to BM2 as a freight broker — Ed referenced their lanes, backhaul network, customers and margins, and probed their specific TMS to frame the integration value.

evidence.quote: back haul lanes fits in your network
we look at your customers, your lanes, your profit margins, and sit there and say like this should be your ICP.
And remind me what TMS you guys are on.

discovery_progression.explanation: Ed asked genuinely productive questions (price per seat, TMS, whether gain-share could justify cost), but the call closed on an open-ended 'we'll send an email' with no scheduled follow-up or date attached to any next step.

evidence.quote: do you know your do you know your average like roughly um price per seat?
is that a possibility where if you make more money, is that an easy way to justify a cost?
We'll follow up with an email with a couple of different options and just bullet points and see what we could do.

Ed Stockman.coaching_action: Before ending the call, lock a specific dated next step — propose an actual follow-up meeting (day and time) to walk through the emailed pricing options and Talmadge's ZoomInfo contract findings, instead of leaving it at 'we'll send an email.'

signals.signal: Strong own-words endorsement of product value across the org
signals.quote: we've gotten nothing but great great feedback across uh the organization and the floor.

signals.signal: Explicit intent to adopt once the contract blocker is cleared
signals.quote: I would rather go the traditional way of seeing what this Zoom Info contract looks like, trying to get out of that and then locking it.

signals.signal: Buyer-owned next step to unblock the deal
signals.quote: I got to do my part in engaging with Zoom Info and seeing, you know, is there an out clause?

signals.signal: Serious-buyer diligence via a substantive data-sharing objection
signals.quote: we've been down that road before.
